coredata.dc:title: Green conversion of delignified sorghum straw and polyethylene glycol into form-stable phase change materials with promising solar energy capture, transition, and storage capabilities
coredata.dc:creator: Ren, Junchao
Lu, Wenyu
Zhang, Fuqiang
Han, Xiangsheng
Cai, Hongzhen
Yang, Keyan
coredata.dc:description: Phase change materials (PCMs) have attracted considerable attention as a thermal energy management technology for thermal storage. However, achieving high energy-storing abilities, low leakage rates, and solar absorption abilities simultaneously in PCMs remains greatly challenging. This research proposed a green strategy for preparing sorghum straw-based PCMs. By facile delignification and solvothermal process, delignified sorghum straw (DSS) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from removal lignin are prepared. The obtained PEG@CQDs/DSS possessed considerable reusable stabilities, excellent photo-thermal conversion properties, and thermal energy management capacities due to the delicate micropores and intrinsic noncovalent interactions among components. Especially, the PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS exhibited superior solar-thermal conversion capabilities (with conducive photo-thermal conversion efficiency ~90.84%), and kept stable after 100 cycles of heating and cooling, in which the melting enthalpy value is ~168.1 J/g (enthalpy efficiency of ~91.11%). In conclusion, the synthesized PCMs showed potential for application in energy-saving and building thermal management.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biochar
Composite film
Thermal stabilities
Sorghum straw-based PCMs

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Phase change performances of composite PCMs
Thermal cyclic stabilities and reliability of PEG@CQDs/DSS
Solar-thermal conversion and storage performance of PEG@CQDs/DSS

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The preparation of CQDs and PEG@CQDs/DSS composites.

SEM images of cross-section of (A) DSS, (B) PEG/DSS, and (C) PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS composite. (D) Survey spectrum, (E) C 1s, and (F) O 1s spectrum of DSS and PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS, respectively. (G) The potential non-covalent interactions in PEG@CQDs/DSS composites.

(A) DSC curves, (B) phase change temperature, (C) phase change enthalpy, (D) CQDs mass, (E) load factor, and (F) enthalpy efficiencies of different composites. (G) DSC curves, (H) XRD patterns, and (I) FT-IR spectra of PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS before and after 100 cycles.
Leakage test of PEG, PEG/DSS, PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS for different time points.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of CQDs, PEG, PEG/DSS, and PEG/CQDs-7.5/DSS. (B) Time-temperature curves and (C) photothermal energy conversion efficiency of PEG/DSS and PEG/CQDs-7.5/DSS. (D) The schematic diagram of setup for solar-thermal conversion test. (E) Comparison with relevant work in terms of solar-thermal conversion efficiency and enthalpy. (F) Temperature-time curves of PEG/DSS and PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS composite.
Comparison of thermal properties of different PCMs in literature.
Thermal properties of PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS composite before and after 100 times heating-cooling cycles.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: Tm, ΔHm, Tc, and ΔHc presented the melting temperature, the melting enthalpy, the crystallization temperature, and the crystallization enthalpy.

head.ce:title: Green conversion of delignified sorghum straw and polyethylene glycol into form-stable phase change materials with promising solar energy capture, transition, and storage capabilities

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Agricultural Engineering and Food Science, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo 255000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Agricultural Engineering and Food Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Agricultural Engineering and Food Science, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo, 255000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Research Center of Engineering and Technology for Clean Energy, Zibo 255000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Research Center of Engineering and Technology for Clean Energy

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shandong Research Center of Engineering and Technology for Clean Energy, Zibo, 255000, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Agricultural Engineering and Food Science, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo 255000, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Agricultural Engineering and Food Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Phase change materials (PCMs) have attracted considerable attention as a thermal energy management technology for thermal storage. However, achieving high energy-storing abilities, low leakage rates, and solar absorption abilities simultaneously in PCMs remains greatly challenging. This research proposed a green strategy for preparing sorghum straw-based PCMs. By facile delignification and solvothermal process, delignified sorghum straw (DSS) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from removal lignin are prepared. The obtained PEG@CQDs/DSS possessed considerable reusable stabilities, excellent photo-thermal conversion properties, and thermal energy management capacities due to the delicate micropores and intrinsic noncovalent interactions among components. Especially, the PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS exhibited superior solar-thermal conversion capabilities (with conducive photo-thermal conversion efficiency ~90.84%), and kept stable after 100 cycles of heating and cooling, in which the melting enthalpy value is ~168.1 J/g (enthalpy efficiency of ~91.11%). In conclusion, the synthesized PCMs showed potential for application in energy-saving and building thermal management.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Phase change materials (PCMs) can store and release thermal energy with tiny variations in temperature as they undergo a phase transition, and achieve the storage of energy materials by exchanging energy with the external environment [1–3]. Because of their high energy density, the constant temperatures in the phase change process, energy output stabilities and other outstanding advantages, the PCMs can be widely used in thermal insulation [4], decoration [5], furniture [6], storage and building energy saving [7,8]. Especially in the building energy-saving industry, PCMs can absorb most heat from external high-temperature environments and releasing it at lower ambient temperatures to regulate the overall temperatures [9,10]. Therefore, the energy efficiency of buildings can be effectively improved using PCMs to capture solar energy from the hot outdoor environment during the day [11]. Additionally, it is an extremely promising strategy to utilize solar energy and protect the environment [12–14].

Nevertheless, the development of PCMs is still limited by several significant problems, including limited storage life [15], weak absorption capacities for solar-to-thermal conversion [10], low thermal conductivity for phonon transmission [16], susceptibilities to liquid leakage during circulation, and poor reliabilities [17–19]. Some highly thermally conductive metallic materials (e.g., boron nitride [20], silver powders [21], and sheet metal materials [22]) have been added to PCMs to mitigate this issue [23,24]. However, initial investigations indicated a competitive relationship between thermal conductivity additives and energy storage density [25,26]. This means that as the additive content increases, the energy storage density decreases [27]. In addition, the thermally conductive additives will inevitably detach from the composite PCMs during thermal cycling due to interfacial effects [28,29]. So large-scale applications in the energy storage field are still restricted by inherent limitations, including complex manufacturing processes, high costs, low yields, and chemical contamination [30,31]. Carbon-based nanomaterials, such as carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanotubes (CNTs), and carbon fibers (CF), have shown excellent sunlight absorption capabilities, low cost, non-toxicities, and stabilities [32–34]. Moreover, they can be easily incorporated into organic materials to improve their structures and properties [35]. Hence, integrating carbon-based nanomaterials (such as CQDs) can enhance the photo-thermal transformation capacities of the carrier materials [36]. This leads to the development of dimensionally stable PCMs composites with high heat storage densities, low leakage rates and high efficiency of photothermal effects [37,38].

This paper proposes PCMs with favorable energy storage abilities, low leakage rates, and solar energy absorption capability for thermal energy storage. Delignified sorghum straw (DSS) with a distinctive grid structure, substantial porosity, and porous structure was successfully prepared by a simple delignification process. In addition, the solvothermal method was used to convert the precursor lignin into CQDs. This resulting PEG@CQDs/DSS exhibited excellent photothermal transformation efficiencies (up to ~90.84% for PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS), an enthalpy of 168.1 J/g, and excellent thermal reliability (kept stable over 100 cycles). This is attributed to the microporous structures and potential interactions (e.g., hydrogen bonding and van der Waals forces) [39]. Therefore, this paper presents novel insights into the fabrication of biomass-based PCMs superior to both solar energy capture, transfer, and energy storage properties.

Sorghum straw (SS) was obtained from the rural in areas of Henan province of China. The SS was washed, dried, and cut into a length of approximately 4.0 cm in the growth direction. Sodium sulfite (Na2SO3, AR), anhydrous ethanol Sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 40%), phosphoric acid (H3PO4), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30% solution) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, AR) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). O-phenylenediamine (OPDA) and PEG (average Mn = 2000) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hydrochloric acid (HCl) was obtained from Taicang Hutex Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

The SS was impregnated by toluene-ethanol reagent mixtures for 48 h to remove waxes and surface impurities, and dried at 60°C for 24 h. Subsequently, the SS was immersed into delignified lignin solutions (2.5 mol/L NaOH and 0.4 mol/L Na2SO3) to boil for 6 h, then rinsed in hot water three times. The brown delignified solutions were collected for further use after cooling to room temperature. Then, the SS samples were bleached in the boiling H2O2 (2.5 mol/L) solution until the yellow color vanished. The treated SS was washed with cold deionized water three times, and stored in ethanol solutions for 12 h. Finally, the DSS was frozen (−20°C) and freeze-dried, as shown in Fig. 1 (step 1).

Lignin was precipitated through the acidic conditions created by the 2 mol/L HCl solutions. After that, the lignin was centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min and subsequently washed until a neutral pH was achieved. Then, the solid was lyophilized under vacuum at −60°C for 24 h. Thereafter, 0.3 g lignin and 0.9 g OPDA were dissolved in mixed solutions of 40% H2SO4 (15 mL) and H3PO4 (15 mL) with stirring for 10 min. The mixtures were heated at 220°C for 10 h in a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (100 mL). After that, the supernatant was obtained by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 15 min. Next, the supernatant underwent dialysis in deionized water (retained molecular weight: 1000 Da) for 48 h to remove small molecules. Finally, the purified solution was dried at 70°C to obtain CQDs, as demonstrated in Fig. 1 (step 2).

First, 30 g of PEG2000 was placed in a beaker at 60°C until it melted. Then 1, 2.5, 5, and 7.5 mL of CQDs solutions (anhydrous ethanol 1.2 mg/mL) were added with continuous stirring for 20 min. Then, the prepared DSS was impregnated with PEG@CQDs solutions and transferred to a vacuum oven at 70°C with a vacuum pressure of −0.1 MPa for 30 min. The composites acquired were labeled PEG@CQDs-1/DSS, PEG@CQDs-2.5/DSS, PEG@CQDs-5/DSS, PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS, respectively. The PEG/DSS was prepared as the control group by impregnating PEG into DSS to compare PEG@CQDs/DSS. as shown in Fig. 1 (step 3).

The morphologies of the samples were examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Quanta250, USA) at an accelerating voltage of 10 kV. The microstructure of CQDs was examined through transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi H-7650, Japan). X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo VGRSCAKAB 250×, USA) with Al-Kα radiation was used to analyze the atomic compositions and chemical functional groups of the samples at room temperature. The structure and phase of the composites were acquired by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8-02, Germany). The functional groups of samples were determined by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet 5700, Thermo Fisher Nicolet, USA) in the form of the KBr pellet over the 400–4000 cm−1 range. The specific surface area and pore size of SS and DSS were evaluated by an N2 adsorption/desorption analyzer (Micromeritics, ASAP 2460, USA) using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method. The data on phase change behavior, including transition enthalpy and temperatures, were acquired using differential scanning calorimetry (DSC, Q2000, USA). The thermal stabilities of the samples were assessed using the thermal gravimetric analyzer (TGA, SDT650, USA) from 30°C to 800°C with a heating rate of 10°C/min under nitrogen flow. The thermal conductivities were determined using a thermal constant analyzer (Hot Disk, TPS2500S, Sweden) at a temperature of 25°C. The measurement method employed was the transient plane source method. A photothermal conversion test was conducted under simulated sunlight provided by a solar simulator (Microenerg, CEM-SL500, China) with an AM 1.5 filter and a 750 nm laser (MRL-III-FS-750 nm-1.0 W, China). The ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of the samples were recorded using a UV–vis-near-infrared (UV–vis-NIR) spectrometer (PE lambda 750, USA).

The preparation processes of PEG@CQDs/DSS composites are shown in Fig. 1, including delignification of SS, preparation of CQDs, and vacuum impregnation of PEG@CQDs. SEM was applied to study the cross-section microstructures of SS before and after chemical treatment. As shown in Figs. 2A and S1, the color of DSS became completely white due to the removal of chromophoric groups from the SS [5,13,14] (illustration in the upper left corner of Fig. 2A). Moreover, the anisotropic porous structures of SS were effectively maintained during delignification. This phenomenon can be verified by the high specific surface area (~14.02 m2 g−1) (Fig. S2) of DSS compared to SS (~6.97 m2 g−1).

The CQDs were prepared by a straightforward hydrothermal approach and their morphology was investigated by TEM (Fig. S3). The mean diameter of the CQDs was 8.85 nm, and the crystal structure of the CQDs was confirmed by high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images, displaying an interlayer spacing of 0.24 nm, corresponding to the (100) lattice plane of graphene [40,41]. The XRD patterns showed a peak at 2θ of 19.27°, attributed to the (002) lattice plane of the graphitic structure [42] (Fig. S4). Generally, when exposed to sunlight, CQDs absorb energy and move from their ground state to an excited state. Then, the radiation emits energy, returning the CQDs to their ground state and producing fluorescence. This phenomenon is known as photoluminescence, resulting from the transition between energy levels, which is responsible for endowing CQDs with superior performance [35]. FT-IR spectroscopy was used to identify the surface groups of CQDs. As depicted in Fig. S5, the stretching vibration of C-OH (3381 cm−1), CH3 (3078), CH2 (2918 cm−1), and CO (1622 cm−1), and others, such as COC at 1450 and 1222 cm−1, CH at 937–1093 cm−1 was detected. The above analysis indicates that the surface of CQDs contained considerable hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, creating a suitable environment for employing them as guest molecules to embellish PEG/DSS [43,44].

It became evident that the surface morphology of PEG/DSS was smooth after loading PEG (Fig. 2B). Meanwhile, the pore structure of DSS was impregnated with PEG, which resulted in reduced visibility of the pores, indicating successful impregnation. Moreover, the encapsulation of PEG/DSS and PEG@CQDs/DSS exhibited negligible variation (Fig. 2C), thus confirming that the morphology of the porous DSS remained unchanged in the presence of nano-scale CQDs (with an average diameter of 8.85 nm). Compared to PEG/DSS, PEG@CQDs/DSS showed a gradual darkening of the color, indicating the successful doping of the CQDs (Fig. S6). However, PEG/DSS and PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS both showed smooth surface morphology, likely because the CQDs were well dispersed (Fig. 2C). Furthermore, the interaction between PEG/DSS and CQDs was reinforced by hydrogen bonding. This effectively entrapped the melting PCMs in the channel during the phase change process, thus preventing leakage [43].

XPS was conducted to analyze the primary interactions of elements on the surface of DSS and PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS mainly displayed elements of C and O (Fig. 2D), to investigate the surface chemical compositions of the DSS and PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS, the relatively high concentrations of C and O indicated a significant presence of polar functional groups. As shown in Fig. 2E, the PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS exhibited peaks at 284.8 eV (CC), 286.5 eV (CO), and 287.8 eV (OCO), which are related DSS. Nevertheless, the intensity of OCO bonding in the PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS spectrum was decreased [45]. Further, the peak at 533 eV (CO) in the O1s spectrum of DSS shifted slightly to 532.6 eV for PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS; the CO bonding of DSS was not observed in PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS and a new peak appeared around 534.1 eV (H2O) for PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS compared with DSS. This indicates the potential hydrogen bonding between DSS and PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS [46] (Fig. 2F). XRD analysis was used to investigate the behavior of the PEG molecules during crystallization. Fig. S7 indicates that PEG/DSS and PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS had a crystalline structure comparable to pure PEG after impregnation with PEG. The results revealed two intense diffraction peaks at 2θ of 21.01° and 23.20°, corresponding to two crystal planes of (001) and (110) [47], respectively. The results affirmed the triumphant integration of PEG molecules in PEG@CQDs/DSS. It is noteworthy that DSS did not alter the crystalline structure of PEG and PEG still showed good crystallization behavior. This suggests a physical interaction between DSS and PEG. The XRD results were verified by additional FT-IR to ensure accuracy (Fig. S8). Peaks were detected at 962 and 2887 cm−1, indicative of the stretching vibration of CH, corresponding to CH2 of PEG. Additionally, peaks were detected at 1114 cm−1 and 3410 cm−1, representing the stretching extension of CO and OH of PEG [15,48], respectively. The FT-IR spectra also revealed peaks associated with PEG, including CO, CH, and OH. Notably, no new distinguishable peaks were observed in the FT-IR spectra of the composite attained. Both the composite PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS and pure PEG exhibited basically identical peak positions, suggesting the presence of solely physical interaction between DSS and PEG molecules. This interaction absorbed PEG into porous channels and effectively prevented leakage of molten PEG. Furthermore, following hydro-thermal fabrication, the CQDs developed a few oxygen-containing groups, and the delignification process of DSS unveiled a greater amount of cellulose-based surface with additional hydroxy groups. Such functional groups can create potent chemical bonds, including hydrogen bonds, which may greatly improve the chemical stability and effectiveness of the compound [49]. The powerful combination of binding is predicted to ensure chemical stability during the thermal storage process of PEG (Fig. 2G).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phase change performances of composite PCMs
ce:section.ce:para:
The thermal storage capabilities of pure PEG and composite PCMs were analyzed via DSC (Fig. 3A). The melting temperature (Tm) and crystallization temperature (Tc) of PEG were 53.06 and 37.28°C (Fig. 3B), respectively, and the melting latent heat (ΔHm) and crystallization latent heat (ΔHc) were 184.5 and 173.5 J/g (Fig. 3C). The elevated enthalpy phase transition, along with the appropriate phase transition temperature confirmed the ideal thermal storage capacity of pure PEG. The fusion of PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS produced a latent heat of 168.1 J/g. This is marginally lower than neat PEG since only PEG loading corresponds to the latent heat, and CQDs replaced a part of PEG (as seen in Fig. 3D). Furthermore, all examples exhibited comparable phase change behaviors compared to neat PEG. The loading rate (ω) of PEG could reach 94.45%, as shown in Fig. 3E, though the addition of CQDs occupied the pore volume of DSS, which was larger than the values published (Table 1 ) [50–57]. The enthalpy efficiency (λ) of PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS was 91.11% (Fig. 3F). The loading rate (ω), enthalpy efficiency (λ), defined by Eqs. (1) and (2) [58,59] are calculated to evaluate the thermal energy capacity of PEG@CQDs/DSS:
(1)
ω =
m 2
−
m 1
m 2
× 100 %
(2)
λ =
ΔH
m − PEG @ CQDs / DSS
ΔH
m − PEG
× 100 %
where m1 is the initial mass of the DSS before the loading the PEG@CQDs, and m2 is the final mass of the PEG@CQDs/DSS after finishing loading the PEG; ΔHm-PEG@CQDs/DSS and ΔHm-PEG represent the melting enthalpy of PEG@CQDs/DSS and PEG, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal cyclic stabilities and reliability of PEG@CQDs/DSS
ce:section.ce:para:
The thermal cycling stability is critical to the practical application of PEG@CQDs/DSS that enables energy storage through the excellent thermal reliability properties of materials. Therefore, the cycling performance was evaluated through 100 thermal cycling tests involving heating and cooling. To calculate the phase change enthalpy loss before and after the thermal cycle, a relative coefficient (η) was introduced according to Eqs. (3)–(4) [15,23].
(3)
η m
=
ΔH mbefore
−
ΔH mafter
ΔH mbefore
× 100 %
(4)
η c
=
ΔH cbefore
−
ΔH cafter
ΔH cbefore
× 100 %
where ΔHmbefore, ΔHmafter, ΔHcbefore, and ΔHcafter were the latent heat of melting and crystallization of PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS composites before and after 100 cycles, respectively.

In the DSC cycles (Fig. 3G), the phase change temperature and latent heat of the PEG@CQDs/DSS (with PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS as an example) were comparable to those of the initial material; the ηm and ηc of PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS were 6.2% and 4.2% (Table 2 ), respectively, indicating its excellent stability in energy storage. The XRD and FT-IR analyses, as seen in Fig. 3H and I, were undertaken to investigate the molecular structure and crystallographic properties of PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS before and after 100 cycles. The position and intensity of characteristic peaks in XRD and FT-IR spectra of PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS remained unchanged. Therefore, these findings indicate that the as-prepared PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS exhibited remarkable thermal reliability and repeatability.

TGA was used to determine the thermal stability of DSS, PEG, CQDs, PEG/DSS and PEG@CQDs/DSS. (Fig. S9). It is evident that the DSS decomposition was initiated at approximately 200°C, and the maximum weight loss rate of DSS was observed at 326°C. After conducting further investigation, it was discovered that the derivative thermogravimetry (DTG) curve for TG did not exhibit multiple peaks. This, implies that the weight loss phase of DSS was comparatively straightforward, with only one step. Thermal degradation of PEG was only a one-step process in the temperature range of 310 to 400°C. For CQDs, two weight loss stages were observed in the TGA curve and corresponding DTG thermograms for CQDs, evidencing the biphasic nature of the weight loss process. This implies that the weight loss occurred in two distinct steps. The CQDs began to lose weight at approximately 60°C owing to the desorption of absorbed water [60], while their genuine degradation temperature at around 375°C. This is because the hydrothermal temperature for preparing of CQDs was 220°C, so the thermal degradation temperature should not be lower than 220°C. For the PEG@CQDs/DSS compound, the TG curve displayed two weight loss stages and the matching DTG thermograms showed two peaks, which are attributed to the degradation of DSS and PEG. In addition, PEG@CQDs/DSS demonstrated minimal weight loss below 200°C, ensuring their suitability for typical usage within the phase transition temperature range, and the PEG@CQDs/DSS exhibited high thermal stability.

Fig. 4 reveals the digital pictures of pure PEG, PEG/DSS, and PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS at 70°C. The pure PEG solid was melted when heated for 15 min, resulting in the spread of liquid PEG on the filter paper. In contrast, only a minor exudation on the filter paper was observed for PEG/DSS after 15 min. It is worth noting that the PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS retained its original shape with no liquid leakage observed. After replacing the filter paper and 30 min of observation, the escape or leakage of PEG/DSS became more visible. In contrast, PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS continued to show exceptional shape stability, like the results of the initial heating. For reference, Fig. S10 displays the mass loss percentage of PEG in PEG/DSS and PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS after 120 min. The porous structure, through surface tension force and capillary action, successfully limited PEG leakage during the melting process, as indicated by an overall leakage rate of 6.51% for PEG/DSS. Comparatively, PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS demonstrated a negligible loss in PEG mass, with 2.95% attributed to its molten state on the sample surface. Consequently, PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS displayed greater dimensional stability than PEG/DSS. It is reasonably inferred that the DSS-based structure could effectively secure PEG molecules through H-bonding interactions with the hydroxyl and amine functional groups present on the surface of CQDs [35]. Furthermore, the permeable structure of the framework could hinder PEG leakage caused by capillarity and surface tension during the melting procedure. Over an extended period, PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS presented remarkable features of form stability. Therefore, it is a reasonable deduction that DSS-CQDs were a suitable substrate for integrating organic phase change materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solar-thermal conversion and storage performance of PEG@CQDs/DSS
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5A shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of CQDs, PEG, DSS/PEG and PEG@CQDs/DSS. The data show that CQDs exhibited strong and extensive light absorption across the entire solar spectrum in the 200–2500 nm range, indicating a high capacity for solar thermal conversion. Conversely, PEG and PEG/DSS showed only minimal absorbance and cannot be considered adequate for photothermal conversion purposes. It is evident that the addition of CQDs remarkably amplified the absorbance of PEG@CQDs/DSS, thereby establishing a solid foundation for its exceptional photothermal conversion efficiency.

An experimental setup was constructed to replicate solar-to-thermal experiments (Fig. 5D), to examine the solar-thermal conversion efficiency of PEG@CQDs/DSS. Specifically, as shown in Fig. 5B, it is evident that the temperature of PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS rose swiftly under simulated sunlight, with an increment of 25 to 50°C in only 200 s. The temperature rise curve exhibited gradually rising trends when the temperature attains the phase-transition point. This phenomenon caused the temperature rise curve to plateau gradually. The PCM transitioned from a solid to a liquid state, resulting in the absorption of a portion of heat and a consequent deceleration in the temperature increase rate. Additionally, the temperature increase rate for pure PEG was meaningfully lower than that of PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS because white PEG reduced its capacity to absorb light. After 960 s of continuous illumination, the temperature of PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS rises to 58.18°C. By contrast, the temperature of PEG/DSS fell short of its melting point, reaching only 43°C. When the light was switched off, the temperature of PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS decreased significantly. Eventually, when the temperature approached the crystallization point, another plateau became apparent, demonstrating that the PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS compound gradually discharged the energy accumulated during the heating phase. Conversely, the graph of PEG/DSS does not show a plateau; because of the low light absorption of PEG/DSS, the phase transition temperature of PEG was not reached in photothermal experiments to the extent that the phase transition process did not occur. The photothermal conversion efficiency of PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS was 90.84% (Fig. 5C), which was a high photo-heat transformation efficiency of the composite compared with related studies as shown in Fig. 5E.

CQDs produced with lignin exhibit excellent light absorption properties and effective photothermal conversion [61]. Thermal conductivity tests were performed on the composites to further evaluate the photothermal conversion capabilities of the PEG@CQDs/DSS. Fig. S11 shows that the thermal conductivity of PEG@CQDs/DSS remained unchanged after adding of CQDs. In addition, the PEG@CQDs/DSS was placed under the 750 nm laser (1 W cm−2) (Fig. 5F). When the surface of PEG@CQDs/DSS was exposed to a 750 nm laser irradiation, a rapid increase was observed in temperature of the composites. As with the photothermal conversion curve, a temperature plateau was present during the phase transition process. Following this, the temperature of the composite rapidly increased. After 90 s of light irradiation, PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS experienced a rise in temperature from room temperature to 78.5°C. No emission peaks were observed on PEG/DSS when subjected to a 750 nm laser. The results demonstrate that the temperature escalation was not because of the laser, but rather caused by the incorporating CQDs, which generated heat through radiation [62]. Notably, a plateau was discernible on the PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS curve at temperatures ranging from 49 to 50°C. This indicates a phase transition between the solid and liquid states of PEG, enabling it to retain radiant heat energy. This indicates that PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS was reaching the crystallization temperature of the PEG, releasing a large amount of stored heat and this heat was used to regulate the surrounding temperature. In comparison, the curve of PEG/DSS does not show a plateau as it possessed a very low light absorption capacity, making it unable to reach the phase transition temperature and undergo the phase transition process. Furthermore, the photo-heating transformation efficiency for PEG/DSS and PEG@CQDs/DSS can be calculated using the below photo-heating calculation equation [12,63]:
(5)
η =
m Δ H
ρS
t t
−
t f
× 100 %

When ΔH represents the enthalpy change during the phase transition, m denotes the weight of the sample, S denotes the surface area of the sample, t
t and t
f are the initial and final times before and after the transition, respectively, and ρ represents the optical power density of the simulated light source. These parameters are calculated using the following formula.

Fig. S12 illustrates the photo-heating response of PEG@CQDs/DSS with varying CQDs compositions. The materials showed comparable heating performance; however, the heating-up rate of PEG@CQDs/DSS increased as the CQDs content increased. This confirms that introducing CQDs significantly improved the photothermal conversion efficiency of PEG@CQDs/DSS.

In summary, a novel shape-stabilized composite PCMs with DSS as supporting material, CQDs derived from the removed lignin as backfill fluorescent material, and PEG as phase change materials was reported. The porous structure of the DSS surface and polar functional groups of CQDs were better at resisting PEG leakage. Therefore, the affordable PCMs produced from PEG@CQDs/DSS exhibited remarkable shape stabilization, enabling the PCMs to harness radiant heat to regulate the surrounding temperature and conserve energy. The PEG@CQDs-7.5/DSS also exhibited efficient photo-thermal conversion efficiency (~90.84%), the high loading rate of ~94.45%, large energy storage capacity (~168.1 J/g), good thermal reliability after 100 heating-cooling cycles, and favorable thermal stability. Benefiting from green preparation strategy, encapsulation capacity, and excellent thermal properties, the PEG@CQDs/DSS composites prepared in this work will be a promising candidate that can be used in thermal management, insulation devices, solar heating, and other applications of buildings.

Junchao Ren: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Wenyu Lu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Fuqiang Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Xiangsheng Han: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Hongzhen Cai: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Keyan Yang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2023QB079), the Key Research and Development Project of Shandong Province (major scientific and technological innovation projects) (No. 2021SFGC0201 and 2021SFGC0203).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2024.129808.
